meta.unit: Big Idea 4: Computer Systems and Networks

map.subtitle: Big Idea 4: Computer Systems and Networks   •   2 days   •   LO CSN-1.A, CSN-1.B, CSN-1.C, CSN-1.D
map.intro: Everything for Topic 4.1 lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key — plus the Innovation Investigation #2 attachment note)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Packet Journey — The Photo That Arrived in Pieces and Exercise 2: Network Diagnosis Clinic (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — No One's in Charge (and It Works Anyway) (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 9 questions across LO CSN-1.A, CSN-1.B, CSN-1.C, and CSN-1.D, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1: devices → systems → networks, paths and routing, bandwidth, and the Internet's open-protocol design; Day 2: streams → chunks → packets, IP/TCP/UDP, and the Internet vs. the World Wide Web, closing with Common AP Traps and the exit check).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'The Internet'; the running examples across both days are the seven-node bell-ringer network (Day 1) and the five-packet photo transfer (Day 2), which Exercise 1 extends with a mid-transfer link failure. Innovation Investigation #2 (a course-resource document) attaches to this topic per the pacing guide. All materials refer to this one topic.

exercise1.title: Packet Journey — The Photo That Arrived in Pieces
exercise1.intro: You are the network engineer reviewing last night's transfer log: one photo, five packets, sent from a laptop to a phone across six devices — with a link failure in the middle of the transfer. For each log entry you will name what traveled, how it was labeled, and why delivery still succeeded. Part A works the log table; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives CSN-1.A, CSN-1.B, and CSN-1.C.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Transfer Log: One Photo, Five Packets, One Dead Link
exercise1.narrative: The network: Laptop L connects to router R1; R1 connects to R2 and R3; R2 and R3 each connect to R4; R4 connects to Phone P. At 8:00 p.m. the laptop sends a 2 MB photo to the phone. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, name each artifact with CED vocabulary (data stream, chunk, packet, metadata, path, routing), and test every claim against what the log actually shows.

rows: 8:00:00 — photo queued to send
The 2 MB photo becomes a data stream, split into 5 chunks; each chunk is wrapped in a packet stamped with destination (P) and sequence number (1–5)
Still on the laptop
No single packet carries the whole photo
8:00:01 — packets #1 and #2 delivered
Both crossed the network and arrived, in order
L → R1 → R2 → R4 → P
Both packets happened to take the same route
8:00:02 — link R2–R4 fails
A connection dies mid-transfer; packet #3, in flight through R2, is lost
(none — the link is down)
Nobody reprograms anything, and no error appears on the laptop
8:00:03 — packets #4 and #5 delivered
Both arrived — #5 first, then #4
L → R1 → R3 → R4 → P
The route changed without any human choosing it; #5 overtook #4
8:00:04 — phone requests #3; it arrives; photo displays
The phone noticed exactly one piece was missing and got it re-sent
L → R1 → R3 → R4 → P
The finished photo is identical to the original

exercise1.scenarioNote: The network extends the Day 2 five-packet worked table with the mid-transfer link failure; all terms and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: at 8:00:02 a link died while the photo was half-sent — why did the laptop show no error and the transfer still finish?
exercise1.partALabel: Part A - Rulings on the Log

partA.q: Row 1: name the data stream, the chunks, and the packets in this log entry — then state the TWO jobs the metadata (destination address + sequence number) will perform. Use every term correctly.
partA.answer: The DATA STREAM is the 2 MB photo's flow of bits leaving the laptop; it is split into 5 CHUNKS of data, and each chunk is encapsulated in a PACKET (EK CSN-1.C.1). The metadata does two jobs (EK CSN-1.C.2): the destination address serves ROUTING — moving each packet between origin L and destination P — and the sequence numbers serve DATA REASSEMBLY — letting the phone rebuild the chunks in the right order. Full credit requires all three terms placed correctly plus BOTH metadata jobs, not just 'extra information on the packet.'

partA.q: Row 2: write out the path packets #1 and #2 used, and explain why 'L → R2 → R4 → P' would NOT be a valid way to record it. Then name the process that chose the path.
partA.answer: The path is L → R1 → R2 → R4 → P: per EK CSN-1.A.5, a path is a sequence of DIRECTLY connected computing devices that begins at the sender and ends at the receiver. 'L → R2 → R4 → P' is invalid as written because L is not directly connected to R2 — every consecutive pair in a path must share a direct connection, so skipping R1 breaks the definition. ROUTING chose the path: the process of finding a path from sender to receiver (EK CSN-1.A.6). Full credit: the valid path + the direct-connection reason + naming routing.

partA.q: Rows 3–4: a link died mid-transfer, yet packets #4 and #5 arrived by a different route 'without any human choosing it.' Name the CED concept that explains this, and state exactly what 'dynamic' means here.
partA.answer: This is DYNAMIC ROUTING: per EK CSN-1.B.5, routing on the Internet is usually dynamic — it is not specified in advance. 'Dynamic' means the path is found at send time, packet by packet, from whatever connections currently work: when R2–R4 died, routing simply found the surviving path through R3. No administrator re-planned anything, and the laptop never needed to know the route changed. (The design principle of building extra paths on purpose — redundancy and fault tolerance — is named in Topic 4.2.) Full credit requires 'not specified in advance' or an equivalent phrase, not just 'it re-routed.'

partA.q: Across rows 2–5 the log shows all three possible packet fates. Identify each fate and the row(s) showing it — then explain how the phone knew that #3, specifically, was missing.
partA.answer: IN ORDER: packets #1 and #2 (row 2) arrived in sequence. OUT OF ORDER: #5 arrived before #4 (row 4). NOT AT ALL: #3 was lost when the link failed (row 3). Per EK CSN-1.C.3, packets may arrive at the destination in order, out of order, or not at all — all three are normal outcomes, not malfunctions. The phone detected the loss from the REASSEMBLY METADATA: sequence numbers 1, 2, 4, and 5 were present, so the numbering showed exactly one gap at #3, and row 5 logs the re-request that filled it. Full credit: all three fates matched to rows plus the sequence-number-gap explanation.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: The link between R4 and the phone has a bandwidth of 8 million bits per second, and the photo is 2 MB (about 16 million bits). What is the FASTEST the photo could cross that link, and why might the real transfer take longer? Use the CED's definition of bandwidth.
partB.answer: Bandwidth is the MAXIMUM amount of data that can be sent in a fixed amount of time (EK CSN-1.A.7), usually measured in bits per second (EK CSN-1.A.8). At the ceiling: 16,000,000 bits ÷ 8,000,000 bits per second = 2 seconds, at best. The real transfer can take longer because bandwidth is a ceiling, not a promise: the link is shared with other traffic, the packets crossed several hops before this link, and one packet had to be re-sent. Full credit: the 2-second floor, 'maximum' identified as the load-bearing word, and at least one concrete reason reality falls short of the ceiling.

partB.q: The laptop and the phone were built by different companies and run different operating systems, yet their packets interoperated perfectly. Explain how, using the CED definitions of protocol and of open protocols.
partB.answer: A PROTOCOL is an agreed-upon set of rules that specify the behavior of a system (EK CSN-1.B.3) — both devices follow the same packet rules, so the brand of hardware is irrelevant: the rules, not the manufacturers, guarantee compatibility. And because the Internet's protocols are OPEN (nonproprietary), any manufacturer can read and implement them, which is what allows users to easily connect additional computing devices to the Internet (EK CSN-1.B.4). Full credit: both definitions APPLIED to the scenario (shared rules → interoperation; openness → any maker can join), not just recited.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: If this had been a live video call instead of a photo, re-sending #3 two seconds later would be useless — that moment of the call has already passed. Of the protocols named in EK CSN-1.C.4, which fits each job (photo vs. live call), and what does each choice trade away?
partB.answer: The photo wants TCP: it numbers the chunks, confirms arrival, and re-requests missing packets — perfect reconstruction, at the cost of waiting for re-sends. The live call fits UDP: it keeps sending without confirmations or re-sends, so a lost packet becomes a brief glitch and the conversation stays live — speed at the cost of completeness. Both ride on IP, which addresses and routes every packet (EK CSN-1.C.4 names IP, TCP, and UDP as common Internet protocols). Strong answers state the trade-off in both directions: TCP buys completeness with delay; UDP buys immediacy with loss.

exercise2.title: Network Diagnosis Clinic
exercise2.subtitle: 5 diagnoses - LO CSN-1.A, CSN-1.B, CSN-1.C, CSN-1.D
exercise2.intro: You are the on-call network consultant. Five confident-sounding claims arrive from around the school, and every one of them is wrong or half-wrong. For each, name the CED concept it touches BY NAME (path, routing, bandwidth, open protocols, dynamic routing, scalability, packets and metadata, Internet vs. Web), correct the claim, and justify the correction with the mechanism. Advanced students also layer a deeper protocol or design connection. Vague 'the network is fine' verdicts score zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): concept named with CED vocabulary + corrected diagnosis + mechanism, for all 5 claims. Enrichment (optional): also add the deeper layer (a protocol-level or design-level connection) for a more complete diagnosis.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every claim
method: Anchor your gut ruling — is the claim right, wrong, or half-right? — before analyzing. Writing your own diagnosis first keeps a confident-sounding claim from swaying you.

Match the claim to the CED idea it touches: device/system/network, path and routing, bandwidth, protocols and openness, dynamic routing, scalability, packets and metadata, or the Internet-vs-Web distinction.
State what is REALLY happening and why, using the CED definition. A verdict without a mechanism is an incomplete diagnosis — 'the Internet just works that way' is never the mechanism.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (the deeper layer): strong diagnoses connect the correction to the design underneath — out-of-order packets point to TCP's sequence numbers; a school-wide outage points to the Internet's centerless, interconnected-networks design; a bandwidth complaint invites the bits-versus-bytes arithmetic; and every Internet-vs-Web mix-up is really a question about layers, with HTTP riding on TCP/IP.
fields: Concept named (which CED idea is in play):
Corrected diagnosis + mechanism (what's really happening and why):

scenarios.text: The school Wi-Fi goes down during first period. A freshman announces: 'The Internet is broken — like, the WHOLE Internet, for everyone. It's probably down worldwide right now.'

key.signs: a worldwide claim built from a purely local symptom. The tell is 'the whole Internet' — the freshman is picturing a single central computer that can fail for everyone at once, when only the school's own connection point failed.
key.mechanism: name the concept — Internet ACCESS versus the Internet itself. Per EK CSN-1.B.2, access depends on connecting a computing device to an Internet-connected device: the school lost ITS link, nothing more. And per EK CSN-1.B.1 the Internet is a network of interconnected networks with no center, so there is no single machine whose failure could take it down worldwide — every other network is still exchanging packets while the school's Wi-Fi is dark.
key.subtype: the centerless design is deliberate: interconnected networks on open protocols mean no owner and no single point of failure for the whole — the design property Topic 4.2 names fault tolerance. Phones on cellular data during the outage prove the diagnosis: same Internet, different access path.

scenarios.text: A family upgrades to a '200 Mbps' internet plan. Dad complains: 'We were promised 200 megabits every second. My 50-megabyte download took six seconds instead of a quarter of one. We should sue.'

key.signs: bandwidth treated as a guarantee, plus megaBITS confused with megaBYTES. The tell is the math — a quarter second only works if 50 MB were 50 megabits — and the word 'promised,' which misreads the CED's key word, MAXIMUM.
key.mechanism: name the concept — bandwidth. Per EK CSN-1.A.7–A.8, bandwidth is the MAXIMUM amount of data that can be sent in a fixed amount of time, measured in bits per second: a ceiling, not a promise. The honest arithmetic: 50 megabytes ≈ 400 megabits; 400 ÷ 200 = 2 seconds at the theoretical ceiling, so six real-world seconds — with a shared link, multiple hops, and any re-sent packets — is unremarkable, not fraud.
key.subtype: the deeper layer is the 8× factor: bandwidth is quoted in bits per second while file sizes are quoted in bytes, so honest comparisons convert first. Exam items lean on this — 'bits per second' appears verbatim in EK CSN-1.A.8, and 'maximum' is the load-bearing word in A.7.

scenarios.text: Mid-match, a gamer's stats overlay shows packets arriving out of order. She posts: 'Proof my ISP's network is defective — packets are supposed to arrive in the order they were sent.'

key.signs: normal behavior read as a defect. The tell is 'supposed to arrive in order' — the CED says the opposite: out-of-order arrival is one of the three EXPECTED fates of a packet, alongside in order and not at all.
key.mechanism: name the concepts — packets, metadata, and dynamic routing. Per EK CSN-1.C.3, packets may arrive in order, out of order, or not at all; because routing is dynamic (EK CSN-1.B.5), packets from one stream can take different paths with different travel times, so shuffled arrival is built into the design. Each packet's metadata serves routing AND reassembly (EK CSN-1.C.2): sequence information lets her machine restore the order perfectly. Nothing is defective — the system is doing exactly what it was designed to do.
key.subtype: the reordering machinery is TCP's sequence numbering — but her game likely runs on UDP, deliberately accepting loss and disorder for lower delay, because a stale packet in a live match is worthless. That is a protocol CHOICE, not a network defect (EK CSN-1.C.4).

scenarios.text: A startup pitches the school district: 'Ditch the open Internet. Our proprietary NetPro protocol is licensed only to our certified devices — a private network whose rules we control end-to-end. It will be the next Internet, but better.'

key.signs: the pitch reverses the exact property that made the Internet win. The tell is 'licensed only to our certified devices' — closed, proprietary rules, where the Internet's defining design is standardized, OPEN, nonproprietary protocols.
key.mechanism: name the concepts — open protocols and scalability. The Internet is interconnected networks using standardized open protocols (EK CSN-1.B.1); openness is what allows users to easily connect additional devices (EK CSN-1.B.4), and that ease is why the Internet could scale: it was designed to be scalable, with scalability defined as the capacity to change in size and scale to meet new demands (EK CSN-1.B.6–B.7). A licensed protocol grows only as fast as one company approves devices — so 'the next Internet' cannot be built from closed rules; the growth engine is the openness.
key.subtype: history already ran this experiment: the closed online services of the 1980s–90s either adopted open TCP/IP or vanished. Private networks have legitimate niches (security-critical control systems), but the moment two networks must cooperate, they interconnect through the open protocols anyway — when cooperation is the product, openness wins.

scenarios.text: A senior's tech-club presentation declares: 'The Web and the Internet are the same thing. Everything online — your games, your smart speaker, your email — runs on the World Wide Web. That's why it's called being ON the web.'

key.signs: two names treated as one thing, then everything Internet-shaped stuffed into the Web. The tell is the example list — a game session, a smart speaker's audio stream, and mail delivery involve no linked pages at all, so they cannot be 'on the Web.'
key.mechanism: name the concept — the Internet versus the World Wide Web. The World Wide Web is a system of linked pages, programs, and files (EK CSN-1.D.1) that speaks HTTP (EK CSN-1.D.2) and USES the Internet (EK CSN-1.D.3) — the network of interconnected networks that moves packets for EVERY service. Games, smart speakers, and email clients use the Internet directly with their own protocols; no browser, pages, or links means no Web involved. The correction in one sentence: the Web is one service ON the Internet, not a synonym for it.
key.subtype: the layered picture makes it exact: HTTP (the Web) rides on TCP, which rides on IP — while the game may run its own protocol over UDP on the very same IP layer. Same Internet underneath, different services on top; exam stems test this with statements lifted nearly verbatim from EK CSN-1.D.1–D.3.

discussion.title: No One's in Charge (and It Works Anyway)
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: The Internet has no CEO, no headquarters, and no master switch — just shared, open rules that nobody is forced to follow, carrying messages chopped into pieces that may arrive shuffled or not at all. Across three short windows in this topic, your group will decide what actually holds a centerless network together, whether a system built on 'pieces may go missing' is genius or duct tape, and what a government that wants to 'shut down the Web' can and cannot actually reach.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Your group just picked the 'best' path from A to F — but did everyone use the same rule? Fewest devices, avoid the crowded ones, most backup options? A hospital, an esports team, and a movie-streamer are choosing routes right now: would they pick the same 'best,' and should the network itself care whose traffic is whose?

Question 2. Nobody owns the Internet, and its rules are open documents anyone may follow — or ignore. So why does every device maker on Earth choose to follow them? What would actually happen to a company that built a device that didn't?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the five-packets worked table
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The postcard protocol
items.text: A 5-page letter is mailed as 5 separate postcards that may arrive shuffled. Your group has 60 seconds to agree on the MINIMUM that must be written on every postcard so the receiver can rebuild the letter perfectly — and detect that one never arrived. Write your minimum list, then check it against the two jobs the CED assigns to packet metadata.

items.label: Task B - Genius or duct tape?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: the Internet chops everything into packets that may arrive out of order or vanish, instead of opening one guaranteed, continuous line per conversation the way old telephone networks did. Which design is 'better' — and better for WHOM? Record your group's position and the strongest cost of the side you chose.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~9 min, after the Internet-vs-Web section
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'shut it down' thought experiment
items.text: A country's leader orders: 'Shut down the Web here.' A tech advisor replies: 'Which one — the Web or the Internet? They're different things, and shutting them down is different too.' Build your group's ruling: what exactly goes dark if only the WEB is blocked (think HTTP and linked pages) versus the whole INTERNET (packets, every protocol, every service) — and what does the difference reveal about where control actually lives in a centerless network? Keep your ruling: it is a warm-up for Innovation Investigation #2, where you will trace how an Internet-scale innovation moves its data over these same layers.
Our group's verdict (what dies with the Web vs. with the Internet + one-sentence justification):
